title: Problem 1 (Problem 1.4 in [1])
statement_text:
Do there exist functions $f_1, f_2 : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$ such that the following hold?
1. If a graph $G$ is $d$-degenerate, then $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq f_1(d)$.
2. If a graph $G$ has minimum degree at least $f_2(d)$, then $\mathrm{HG}(G) \geq d$.
context_text: Intuitively, very sparse graphs should not allow winning with arbitrarily many colours, and graphs with sufficiently high minimum degree should. This problem, raised in [1] and [9], asks in effect whether the hat guessing number is an equivalent parameter to the degeneracy. It was proved in [13] that there exist $d$-degenerate graphs with hat guessing number as large as $2^{2^{d-1}}$, showing $f_1$ must grow at least doubly-exponentially if it exists.
paper_contribution: Provides positive evidence for part (1.) by introducing strong degeneracy and proving that every strongly $d$-degenerate graph $G$ satisfies $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq (2d)^d$ (Theorem 1.2), together with an exact characterisation of graph classes with bounded strong degeneracy (Theorem 1.3) covering outerplanar graphs, $C_4$-free planar graphs, $K_{2,s}$-free graphs of bounded expansion, and sparse Erdős–Rényi random graphs.
notes: Restated verbatim as Problem 1 in this paper; originally Problem 1.4 in reference [1]. PDF source — some LaTeX may be garbled.
paper_title: Hat guessing numbers of strongly degenerate graphs

title: Open Problem (constant bound on hat guessing number of planar graphs)
statement_text: Whether or not planar graphs allow for a constant bound on their hat guessing number.
context_text: Stated explicitly in [2, 4, 6]. The best known lower bound for the hat guessing number of planar graphs is 14. Bradshaw [6] proved that outerplanar graphs have hat guessing number at most $2^{125000}$ and suspected that there exist outerplanar graphs with hat guessing number as large as 1000.
attributed_to: Alon and Chizewer, Bosek et al., Bradshaw
paper_contribution: Proves $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq 40$ for every outerplanar graph $G$ (Theorem 1.4), drastically improving Bradshaw's bound of $2^{125000}$; also proves $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq 14^7 \approx 10^8$ for every $C_4$-free planar graph $G$.
notes: PDF source — some LaTeX may be garbled.
paper_title: Hat guessing numbers of strongly degenerate graphs

title: Open Problem (hat guessing number bounded by Hadwiger number)
statement_text: Whether the hat guessing number of graphs can be bounded in terms of their Hadwiger number $h(G)$.
context_text: The Hadwiger number $h(G)$ is the largest $t \geq 1$ such that $G$ contains a $K_t$-minor. Alon and Chizewer [2] raised this question; it was also observed in [2] that there exist graphs with no $K_t$-minor whose hat guessing number equals $2^{2^{t-3}}$.
paper_contribution: Gives a positive answer for $K_{2,s}$-free graphs: every $K_{2,s}$-free graph with no $K_t$-minor is strongly $d$-degenerate with $d = O(st\sqrt{\log t})$, so $\mathrm{HG}(G) \leq 2^{O(st\sqrt{\log t}\,\log(st))}$ (Corollary 1.5).
notes: PDF source — some LaTeX may be garbled.
paper_title: Hat guessing numbers of strongly degenerate graphs
